title: practical english usage - Part 293
content:
These meetings can last as long as four hours.

Before a number, as long as can be used to suggest great length.

3 emphatic       use that you drive carefully.) You can take my car as/so long as you drive carefully. (= ... on condition As/so long as is often used to state conditions.

For other conjunctions which  are used in this way, ►    231.

I'll remember that day as long as I live, (     ..

After as long as, we use a present tense to express a future idea.

379 as   long     as For the difference between like and as, ►    515.

He sat there smiling like it u/as his birthday.

It seems like it's going to rain.

in British English.

style. This used to be typically American English, but it is now common Like is often used in the same way as as if/though, especially in an informal 3 like    meaning       'as  if/though' vocabulary •  383    ask ask a favour of somebody and ask (for) permission.

Note also the expressions ask a lot of somebody, ask too much  of somebody, 'How much  is the car? 'I'm asking fifteen hundred.' They're asking £500  a month including tax.

especially in connection with buying, selling and renting.

Ask is sometimes used without for when talking about asking for sums of money, Ask the price.

- Ask for the menu.

Don't ask me my name. (More common than Don't ask me for my name.) not - Don't ask me for money. (     Don't ask me money?) Compare: ask without for: ask somebody to tell something ask for. ask somebody to give something 1 ask    and   ask    for 383 ask For as well, also and too, ►    369.

She sings as well as she plays the piano. (= Her singing is as good She sings as well as playing the piano. (= She not only plays, but also sings.) Note the difference between: / have to feed the animals as well as look after the children.

After an infinitive in the main clause, an infinitive without to is possible.

As well as breaking his leg he hurt his arm. (     ... as well as he broke makes you smell bad?) not Smoking is dangerous, as well as making you smell bad. (     ... as well as it When  we  put a verb after as well as, we  most often use the -ing form.

2 verbs     after    as  well     as Italian in Italy as well as parts of Switzerland.) not   hey They speak Italian in parts of Switzerland as well as Italy. (     T        speak some mammals.) not As well as birds, some mammals can fly. (     Birds can fly, as well as with as well as.

When  some information is already known to the listener/reader, we  put this She works in television as well as writing children's books.

He's clever as well as nice. (= He's not only nice, but also clever.) She's got a goat, as well as five cats and three dogs.

As well as has a similar meaning to 'not only ... but also'.

382 as   well     as Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  384   at, on and in: place at a lecture at the match at a party at a meeting at a concert At is also used before die names of group activities.

He's a student at Oxford. He lives in Cambridge.

We   use at with the name of a city to talk about the city's university. Compare: He's at the London School of Economics.

At is used to say where people study.

She works in a big insurance company.

- She works at Legal and General Insurance.

I first met your father in a shop.

- I first met your father at/in Harrods.

organisations. Compare: At is particularly common with proper names used for buildings or Sorry I didn't phone last night -1 was at the theatre.

Eat at the Steak House - best food in town.

I first heard her sing at the Usher Hall in Edinburgh.

the building itself but of the activity that happens there.

We   very often use at before the name of a building, when we  are thinking not of It was warm and comfortable in the club, (a place to spend time) - Let's meet at the club, (a meeting point) not She lives in Frankfurt, (somebody’ s home) (     She hues at Frankfurt.) - The plane stops for an hour at/in Frankfurt, (a point on a journey) a stage on a journey or a meeting place, for example. Compare: Sometimes we  use at with a larger place, if we  just think of this as a point: Turn right at the next corner.
